items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m again tormented
by vague suspicions…
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the envoy has a medallion…
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the mock-heroic phrasing of «терзают смутные сомнения» reasonably well with “tormented by vague suspicions,” keeping the elevated melodramatic tone that makes the line funny in context. However, it misses the key semantic point noted in the reference: «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» here refers to what each has LOST/what is associated with each theft, not what they currently possess. Rendering it as “Shpak has… the envoy has…” weakens the logic of Bunsha’s ‘connecting the dots’ and blunts the callback humor.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
So nice, Tsar!
So nice—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, so nice!
Hello, Tsar! So nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation keeps the core comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness (“So nice… hello…”) that clashes with the historical setting and Bunsha’s supposed royal role. It also preserves the breathless, drunken redundancy. What it loses is the self-introduction nuance implied in the reference (“I’m the Tsar”), i.e., Bunsha presenting “Tsar” as if it were his name/title in a modern social greeting. Still, the effect remains largely intact.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and function to the approved rendering; preserves the sudden authoritarian, status-drunk persona shift. No loss of humor or clarity.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Faithful and stylistically appropriate: elevated/archaic melancholy in a mundane Soviet setting is preserved. “I’m in sorrow” keeps the slightly formal, theatrical feel. No meaningful issues.

items.translation:
They walled me in!
They walled me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Captures the core misunderstanding and the accusatory “demons” as agent. Slight punctuation/ellipsis difference doesn’t matter for comprehension or comic effect. No issues.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is correct and the religious framing is preserved. However, the reference’s “Behold the power…” better matches the Tsar’s grand, proclamatory register; “So that’s what … can do” is a bit more casual/modern and slightly reduces the mock-solemn ‘miracle’ flourish. Still understandable and funny.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is comprehensible and functions as a command to start dancing, but it loses the specific Soviet/MC-style catchphrase flavor of «Танцуют все!» (a set, showman-like formula). “Everybody dance!” is more generic and less quotable; it also sounds slightly unidiomatic compared to “Everybody’s dancing!” / “Everyone dance!” / “Everybody, dance!” The comedic ‘announcer’ vibe is weakened.

items.translation: What a lucky time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Conveys the opportunistic satisfaction and situational irony (he’s pleased with his own criminal luck). “Lucky time to drop by” is idiomatic and keeps the upbeat tone. Slightly less punchy/quotable than the reference, but the comic intent survives well.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar…
And! Exotic caviar…

Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: This misses the key creative constraint: the joke depends on NOT calling the eggplant spread “caviar” in English, because English viewers will take “eggplant caviar” literally and the inversion of status symbols becomes less clear. The translation explicitly says “Exotic caviar… Eggplant!”, which both (a) keeps “caviar” as the head noun and (b) makes “eggplant” sound like a type of caviar rather than a cheap spread being absurdly elevated. This significantly damages the satire of Soviet scarcity and the punchline structure.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor—

all gone: 3 tape recorders,
3 film cameras,

3 domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list structure and escalating-count humor are partly preserved, but the crucial comic beat is broken: Shpak’s final “куртка… замшевая… Три” is a self-incriminating, absurd afterthought where he ends up with “a suede jacket… three,” implying the count keeps inflating and he’s not even tracking it properly. The translation turns this into “three suede jackets,” which over-specifies and removes the stuttering, suspicious ‘oh—and a jacket… three’ rhythm. It also eliminates the comedic ambiguity of whether he’s lying/exaggerating in the moment. This is a major loss of the gag mechanics.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Matches the reference essentially verbatim in meaning and cadence; preserves the calm, sing-song resignation and the shared-absurdity punch. No issues.

summary.interpretation: 3.55 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.71 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
